perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees and does waive trial by jury in any action, proceeding or counterclaim brought against the other Party for any matter whatsoever arising out of or in any way connected with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that any action, proceeding or counterclaim brought against the other Party for any matter whatsoever arising out of or in any way connected with this Agreement shall be resolved through arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: This clause originally included a jury trial waiver. By removing the waiver and mandating only arbitration, it potentially violates the consumer's right to a jury trial, which, while waivable, requires a knowing and voluntary waiver. Simply stating disputes will be resolved by arbitration without properly addressing the jury trial right can be problematic.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Seventh Amendment to the United States Constitution
perturbation.law_explanation: The Seventh Amendment guarantees the right to a jury trial in civil cases. While this right can be waived, the waiver must be knowing and voluntary. Replacing the original clause with a mandatory arbitration clause without an explicit and informed waiver of the jury trial right could be challenged as an unconstitutional infringement on this right, especially in disputes involving significant amounts of money. Arbitration clauses are generally enforceable but are scrutinized to ensure they don't deprive individuals of fundamental rights without informed consent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Seventh Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Seventh Amendment The Seventh Amendment continues a practice from English common law of distinguishing civil claims which must be tried before a jury (absent waiver by the parties) from claims and issues that may be heard by a judge alone. It only governs federal civil courts and has no application to civil courts set up by the states when those courts are hearing only disputes of state law . Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment VII In suits at common law, where the value in controversy shall exceed twenty dollars, the right of trial by jury shall be preserved, and no fact tried by a jury, shall be otherwise reexamined in any court of the United States, than according to the rules of the common law. Wex Resources Criminal Law Criminal Procedure Jury Other Resources US Code: Title 18 Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure ‹ Sixth Amendment up Eighth Amendment › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the jury trial waiver and mandates arbitration. The Seventh Amendment, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, preserves the right to a jury trial in civil cases. While this right can be waived, the explanation suggests the removal of the original waiver and replacement with mandatory arbitration, without explicit informed consent, creates a legal contradiction as it potentially infringes on the Seventh Amendment right to a jury trial.

perturbation.original_text: Customer will comply with all international, federal, state and local laws, rules, and regulations of any governmental or regulating authority ("Law") that are applicable to its business and use of the Service.
perturbation.changed_text: Customer will comply with all international laws, rules, and regulations of any governmental or regulating authority ("Law") that are applicable to its business and use of the Service.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "federal, state and local" this clause no longer explicitly requires compliance with US laws. Depending on the service provided, this could violate various consumer protection laws at the state or federal level.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FTC Act prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in commerce. By removing the explicit requirement for the Customer to comply with federal laws, the contract creates a loophole where the customer could potentially engage in practices that violate the FTC Act, as the contract no longer clearly mandates adherence to these laws. This creates a risk for Kubient, as it could be argued that they are enabling or facilitating illegal activity by not requiring explicit compliance with US federal laws.

Español Report Fraud Get Consumer Alerts Search the Legal Library Federal Trade Commission Act Tags: Consumer Protection Competition Appliances Alcohol Automobiles Clothing and Textiles Finance Franchises, Business Opportunities, and Investments Funerals Jewelry Real Estate and Mortgages Tobacco Advertising and Marketing Children Endorsements, Influencers, and Reviews Environmental Marketing Health Claims Made in USA Online Advertising and Marketing Telemarketing Advertising and Marketing Basics Credit and Finance Credit and Loans Debt Debt Collection Mortgages Payments and Billing Privacy and Security Children's Privacy Consumer Privacy Credit Reporting Data Security Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act Red Flags Rule Mission Competition Consumer Protection Law 15 U.S.C. §§ 41-58, as amended Links https://www.ftc.govhttp://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml Files File Safe Web Act (194.69 KB) The Federal Trade Commission Act is the primary statute of the Commission. Under this Act, as amended, the Commission is empowered, among other things, to (a) prevent unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce; (b) seek monetary redress and other relief for conduct injurious to consumers; (c) prescribe rules defining with specificity acts or practices that are unfair or deceptive, and establishing requirements designed to prevent such acts or practices; (d) gather and compile information and conduct investigations relating to the organization, business, practices, and management of entities engaged in commerce; and (e) make reports and legislative recommendations to Congress and the public. A number of other statutes listed here are enforced under the FTC Act. Return to top
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text removes the explicit requirement for the customer to comply with federal, state, and local laws, which directly contradicts the FTC Act. The original text ensures adherence to all levels of laws, while the changed text omits federal laws, potentially creating a loophole. As per Scraped Law Snippet 2, the Federal Trade Commission Act empowers the FTC to 'prevent unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce.' By removing the explicit obligation to comply with federal laws, the modified contract undermines this prevention, allowing the customer to potentially engage in practices that violate the FTC Act.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient will protect the Confidential Information, for one (1) year from the date of disclosure, by using at least the same degree of care as it uses to protect its own Confidential Information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent unauthorized use, disclosure or publication.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will protect the Confidential Information, for one (1) year from the date of disclosure, by using a reasonable degree of care, to prevent unauthorized use, disclosure or publication.
perturbation.explanation: This clause reduces the standard of care for protecting confidential information. While reasonable care is mentioned, removing the 'same degree of care as it uses to protect its own Confidential Information' clause weakens the protection. Depending on the type of information being shared (e.g., consumer data), this could violate data protection laws requiring a higher standard of care.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Business Law § 899-AA requires businesses to implement and maintain reasonable security measures to protect private information. Removing the requirement to use the same degree of care as used to protect its own confidential information (but no less than reasonable care) could be seen as a violation of the requirement to maintain reasonable security measures, especially if the recipient's own security measures are significantly stronger than what is considered merely 'reasonable'. This could lead to liability in the event of a data breach where consumer information is compromised due to inadequate security practices.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated the recipient to protect confidential information with at least the same degree of care they use for their own, but no less than reasonable care. The modified version removes the 'same degree of care' requirement, only stipulating 'reasonable care.' The Law Explanation indicates that under New York General Business Law § 899-AA, businesses must maintain reasonable security measures. While the modified contract still mentions 'reasonable care,' the removal of the higher standard (same degree as own confidential info) weakens the protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 points to NYS General Business Law Article 39-F concerning data security protections under SECTION 899-BB. Therefore, the removal of the higher standard of care, could create a legal loophole and a potential violation of the requirement to maintain reasonable security measures, creating a legally relevant contradiction.
